title: SkyHub
description: Full-stack Hypixel SkyBlock stats platform (successor to SkyStats, 15,000+ MAU): 42-endpoint FastAPI backend with PostgreSQL and background workers for leaderboard ranking and networth calculation across 40+ item upgrade types. Self-hosted on a Mac Mini via Docker Compose with Cloudflare Zero Trust tunnel, launchd auto-start, and automated 6-hour PostgreSQL backups.

blog.subtitle: Rebuilding a stats platform on a foundation that could actually scale

sections.heading: Why relational
paragraphs: Hypixel SkyBlock stats trackers are a crowded category, but most of them are built the same way: fetch the Hypixel API response for a player, cache the blob, display it. SkyHub started from a different premise, where the underlying data is relational and modeling it that way from the beginning changes what you can build on top. Player is a row, profile is a row, item is a row, skill is a row. The global leaderboard is a single query instead of a scan across thousands of cached documents. That architecture decision is what made the market data features, leaderboards, and networth calculation tractable.

sections.heading: Design and build
paragraphs: Before writing any code I laid out seven phases in the first commit: foundation, market data pipeline, public API endpoints, items and wiki, player profiles, leaderboards, and events. Each phase had clear acceptance criteria and a defined set of things I expected to learn by the end of it. The whole backend landed in twelve days because having that spec meant decisions about scope were already made before they came up, and each day's work had a clear deliverable.

sections.heading: Networth calculation
paragraphs: Hypixel SkyBlock has more than forty item upgrade types, including reforges, hot potato books, master stars, art of war scrolls, and recombobulators, and the networth calculation walks all of them, stacks correctly for upgrades that don't stack, and prices each one against a rolling bazaar window. On the request path this takes multiple seconds, so it runs as a background worker on a schedule and writes to a cached column on the profile row. The endpoint reads that column. The calculation didn't get faster, the slow part just moved somewhere the user doesn't notice.

sections.heading: Game data edge cases
paragraphs: Three days of the build were spent on game data that doesn't behave the way the API documentation implies. Hypixel rank is a typed enum so downstream views can switch on it safely rather than comparing strings. Pet max levels are dynamic because different pet tiers cap at different levels. Skill caps are dynamic too, because farming and taming are uncapped past certain perks, so overflow XP became its own column. Bank balance was at `member.profile.bank_account`, not on the top-level profile blob. Leather armor dye colors are stored as packed integers at `tag.display.color` inside each item's NBT, parsed out and cast to hex strings, with animated dyes handled separately client-side because they cycle on a timer.

sections.heading: Infrastructure
paragraphs: The backend runs on a Mac Mini on my desk, with Docker Compose orchestrating the FastAPI and Postgres containers and a Cloudflare Zero Trust tunnel carrying public traffic to localhost without exposing a port. A launchd LaunchDaemon plist brings the stack up on boot. A cron inside the Postgres container runs pg_dump every six hours and rotates through seven snapshots. The choice to self-host was deliberate: the operational experience of managing a real deployment is worth more than the convenience of a managed platform, and the cost difference at this scale is significant.

The most instructive bug was a 502 that appeared on roughly one in ten profile refresh calls. The cause was a race between the Hypixel API response and the local cache write, where the client could read mid-state. The fix was a retry loop scoped specifically to that fetch call rather than the whole request handler, which eliminated the failure class without masking legitimate errors elsewhere.
